title: Equirectangular projection

infobox.value: (also called the **equidistant cylindrical projection** or **la carte parallélogrammatique projection**), which includes the special cases of the [Gall isographic projection](/wiki/Gall_isographic_projection "Gall isographic projection") and the **plate carrée projection** (also called the **geographic projection**, **lat/lon projection**, or **plane chart**), is a simple [map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection") attributed to [Marinus of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre") who, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") claims, invented the projection about AD 100.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

*Flattening the Earth: Two Thousand Years of Map Projections*, [John P. Snyder](/wiki/John_P._Snyder "John P. Snyder"), 1993, pp. 5–8, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-226-76747-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-226-76747-7 "Special:BookSources/0-226-76747-7").

Farkas, Gábor. ["Plate Carrée - a simple example"](https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/practical-gis/9781787123328/Text/b21938a9-09f7-46fa-b905-58a0a4ed7d8f.xhtml). *O’Reilly Online Learning*. Retrieved 31 December 2022.

Paul A. Longley; Michael F. Goodchild; David J. Maguire; David W. Rhind (2005). [*Geographic Information Systems and Science*](https://books.google.com/books?id=-FbVI-2tSuYC&pg=PA119). John Wiley & Sons. p. 119. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780470870013](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780470870013 "Special:BookSources/9780470870013").

*Flattening the Earth: Two Thousand Years of Map Projections*, John P. Snyder, 1993, pp. 7, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-226-76747-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-226-76747-7 "Special:BookSources/0-226-76747-7").
["Equidistant Cylindrical (Plate Carrée)"](https://proj.org/operations/projections/eqc.html). *PROJ coordinate transformation software library*. Retrieved 25 August 2020.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Cylindrical equidistant map projection

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Equirectangular_projection_SW.jpg/500px-Equirectangular_projection_SW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equirectangular_projection_SW.jpg)

Equirectangular projection of the world; the standard parallel is the equator (plate carrée projection).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Plate_Carr%C3%A9e_with_Tissot%27s_Indicatrices_of_Distortion.svg/500px-Plate_Carr%C3%A9e_with_Tissot%27s_Indicatrices_of_Distortion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Plate_Carr%C3%A9e_with_Tissot%27s_Indicatrices_of_Distortion.svg)

Equirectangular projection with [Tissot's indicatrix](/wiki/Tissot%27s_indicatrix "Tissot's indicatrix") of deformation and with the standard parallels lying on the equator

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Blue_Marble_2002.png/500px-Blue_Marble_2002.png)](/wiki/File:Blue_Marble_2002.png)

True-colour satellite image of Earth in equirectangular projection

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/World_elevation_map.png/500px-World_elevation_map.png)](/wiki/File:World_elevation_map.png)

[Height map](/wiki/Height_map "Height map") of planet Earth at 2km per pixel, including oceanic [bathymetry](/wiki/Bathymetry "Bathymetry") information, normalized as 8-bit grayscale. Because of its easy conversion between x, y pixel information and lat-lon, maps like these are very useful for software map renderings.

The **equirectangular projection** (also called the **equidistant cylindrical projection** or **la carte parallélogrammatique projection**), which includes the special cases of the [Gall isographic projection](/wiki/Gall_isographic_projection "Gall isographic projection") and the **plate carrée projection** (also called the **geographic projection**, **lat/lon projection**, or **plane chart**), is a simple [map projection](/wiki/Map_projection "Map projection") attributed to [Marinus of Tyre](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre") who, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") claims, invented the projection about AD 100.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The projection maps [meridians](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") to vertical straight lines of constant spacing (for meridional intervals of constant spacing), and [circles of latitude](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude") to horizontal straight lines of constant spacing (for constant intervals of [parallels](/wiki/Circle_of_latitude "Circle of latitude")). The projection is neither [equal area](/wiki/Equal-area_projection "Equal-area projection") nor [conformal](/wiki/Conformal_map_projection "Conformal map projection"). Because of the distortions introduced by this projection, it has little use in [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") or [cadastral](/wiki/Cadastral "Cadastral") mapping and finds its main use in [thematic mapping](/wiki/Thematic_map "Thematic map"). In particular, the plate carrée has become a standard for global [raster datasets](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system"), such as [Celestia](/wiki/Celestia "Celestia"), [NASA World Wind](/wiki/NASA_World_Wind "NASA World Wind"), the [USGS](/wiki/USGS "USGS") [Astrogeology Research Program](/wiki/Astrogeology_Research_Program "Astrogeology Research Program"), and [Natural Earth](/wiki/Natural_Earth "Natural Earth"), because of the particularly simple relationship between the position of an [image pixel](/wiki/Pixel "Pixel") on the map and its corresponding geographic location on Earth or other spherical solar system bodies. In addition it is frequently used in panoramic photography to represent a spherical panoramic image.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equirectangular_projection&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

The forward projection transforms spherical coordinates into planar coordinates. The reverse projection transforms from the plane back onto the sphere. The formulae presume a [spherical model](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth") and use these definitions:

* ![{\displaystyle \lambda }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b43d0ea3c9c025af1be9128e62a18fa74bedda2a) is the [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") of the location to project;
* ![{\displaystyle \varphi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/33ee699558d09cf9d653f6351f9fda0b2f4aaa3e) is the [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") of the location to project;
* ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d7daf493c8f6ef669c04c7b9715532fc35d12d60) are the standard parallels (north and south of the equator) where the scale of the projection is true;
* ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6cf63e8f16cefbe25d3d028fbb464a8c4a53cc7b) is the central parallel of the map;
* ![{\displaystyle \lambda _{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cfa5ad1eb6cdaf3d8dfd77991ee9ce7bdf169184) is the central meridian of the map;
* ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4) is the horizontal coordinate of the projected location on the map;
* ![{\displaystyle y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b8a6208ec717213d4317e666f1ae872e00620a0d) is the vertical coordinate of the projected location on the map;
* ![{\displaystyle R}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4b0bfb3769bf24d80e15374dc37b0441e2616e33) is the radius of the globe.

Longitude and latitude variables are defined here in terms of radians.

### Forward

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equirectangular_projection&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Forward")]

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}x&=R(\lambda -\lambda _{0})\cos \varphi _{1}\\y&=R(\varphi -\varphi _{0})\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/306e3c366e56a773c669f9c79feaf32f2b50ba8a)

The *plate carrée* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), for *flat square*),[[3]](#cite_note-3) is the special case where ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d7daf493c8f6ef669c04c7b9715532fc35d12d60) is zero. This projection maps *x* to be the value of the longitude and *y* to be the value of the latitude,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and therefore is sometimes called the latitude/longitude or lat/lon(g) projection. Despite sometimes being called "unprojected",[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] it is actually projected.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

When the ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d7daf493c8f6ef669c04c7b9715532fc35d12d60) is not zero, such as [Marinus](/wiki/Marinus_of_Tyre "Marinus of Tyre")'s ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}=36^{\circ }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ccf1dfd35a75a45cb7c0649542d5514f1e2a1db0),[[5]](#cite_note-5) the [Gall isographic projection](/wiki/Gall_isographic_projection "Gall isographic projection")'s ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}=45^{\circ }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/749bec67eca0f8e6e62da084497a438230a9f88b), or Ronald Miller's ![{\displaystyle \varphi _{1}=(37.5^{\circ },43.5^{\circ },50.5^{\circ })}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8f619e370546be33dc9110b07e304952b47afaa6),[[6]](#cite_note-6) the projection can portray particular latitudes of interest at true scale.

While a projection with equally spaced parallels is possible for an ellipsoidal model, it would no longer be equidistant because the distance between parallels on an ellipsoid is not constant. More complex formulae can be used to create an equidistant map whose parallels reflect the true spacing.

### Reverse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equirectangular_projection&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reverse")]

:   ![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\lambda &={\frac {x}{R\cos \varphi _{1}}}+\lambda _{0}\\\varphi &={\frac {y}{R}}+\varphi _{0}\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/111a15fecc1eba62e326abadf09669dbec467eb2)

### Alternative names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equirectangular_projection&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Alternative names")]

In spherical panorama viewers, usually:

* ![{\displaystyle \lambda }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b43d0ea3c9c025af1be9128e62a18fa74bedda2a) is called "yaw";[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* ![{\displaystyle \varphi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/33ee699558d09cf9d653f6351f9fda0b2f4aaa3e) is called "pitch";[[8]](#cite_note-8)

where both are defined in degrees.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Equirectangular_projection&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography")

* [Gall–Peters projection](/wiki/Gall%E2%80%93Peters_projection "Gall–Peters projection") (mentions a resolution rejecting the use of all rectangular world maps)
* [List of map projections](/wiki/List_of_map_projections "List of map projections")
* [Mercator projection](/wiki/Mercator_projection "Mercator projection")
* [360 video projection](/wiki/360_video_projection "360 video projection")
* [Wikimedia Gallery of Equirectangular World Maps](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Maps%20of%20the%20world%20with%20equirectangular%20projection)
